sentences.text: My name is Henry Kreisel, K-R-E-I-S-E-L.
I was born in Vienna, Austria in 1929.
April 26th, 1929.
I'm the oldest.
There's two of us.
I have a younger sister named Gerda.
She was born in 1930.
She is a year and a half younger.
My parents-- I left there when I was 10 years old or 10 and 1/2 years old.
And I do remember my parents.
They were good people.
Names were-- my father's name was Michael.
My mother's name was [?
Minah. ?]
The Hebrew-- the Hebrew name is Mickha'el, the Hebrew name.
And the-- and the name of my mother was Myshka.
I think it's a Polish name, Polish name.
My father was born in-- in 1886.
And my mother was born in 1895.
My parents had a-- a grocery store at one time.
Then they had a haberdashery store.
And then I guess a result of the depression, they-- the store went under.
And my father worked for my uncle in a-- in my uncle's haberdashery store.
Yes.
My-- my parents were Orthodox, 'cause I remember going to shul with my father.
Well, I can't describe in terms of community, 'cause I wasn't thinking in terms of community.
My schedule was I went to a regular public school in the daytime and I went to a cheder, a Jewish school, after-- after the regular public school.
So that, in addition to playing with other kids, took care of my-- my social life, and going to shul with my father.
I experienced quite a bit of anti-Semitism.
There was in Vienna-- there was various-- a lot of anti-Semitism before Hitler.
Hitler didn't create anti-Semitism either in Germany or Austria or any other part of the world.
He only took advantage of it.
When I was a kid, I remember the kids calling me Jew boy, before-- before-- before Hitler came in.
And then we had-- we had-- they had classes, religious classes, or religious moments, whatever it was, in the school.
And the Jewish children went out during that period.
Another boy and myself went out of class, left the class during that period.
In this particular class, there were just two of us.
And this was not a-- a Jewish neighborhood specifically.
There were Jewish neighborhoods in Vienna.
And-- and kids used to call you Jew boy.
And sort of in a sense, you're a little bit-- I don't think they were beating up on me then, at that point, before Hitler came.
But they sort of-- you know, it made a difference known, particularly around Christmas, when all that hoopla, Christmas hoopla, which was much more than here in America.
The kids were dressed in red, like little devils or little red jumpers and things.
They were skating on the streets and throwing snowballs.
And so you got into more of a Christmas atmosphere in Vienna, more than over here.
In a sense, yes.
Yes.
In a sense, yes.
Well-- well, after Hitler came, of course, I was just-- and then when Hitler came in '38, I was barely nine years old.
So after Hitler came, the-- the children became more aggressive towards Jews.
You could be beaten up in the street for being a Jew.
And I remember trying to-- to stay in the adults that are walking to-- for protection.
And sometimes it would work.
And sometimes adults would pull away and wouldn't give a damn, would just let the other kids beat up on the Jewish children or assault the Jewish children, taunt and physically assault.
And that was-- sort of went on, went on all the time.
And-- and then in the beginning, we still went to regular school there, to the regular classes.
And then they changed that and the Jews went to different schools.
I don't recall them ever explaining it to me.
It became quite apparent what was going on.
I don't know whether they knew much more about it than I did.
I don't know if anybody could explain it.
It wasn't a Jewish school.

It may have been a public school where they-- where they-- where they put the Jewish children in a public school or in different classes from the regular children, from the other children after a while.
Well, you know, when you're a kid, 9 and 10 years old, you don't-- you don't think in terms of spare time on what you do.
So later on, after Hitler came, I joined the-- a Jewish Zionist organization.
I can't think of the name right now.
Yeah.
Hashomer Hadati.
Hashomer Hadati, which is part of the Mizrachi movement.
And I also joined the Maccabi, which was an exercise-- a health sports club.
So I was involved in these two organizations.
What sort of things did you do with Hashomer Hadati?
We had meetings and talked about things and we sang songs, you know.
And just, you know-- just-- it was something like B'nei Akiva.
It was just like you felt a sense of belonging, of being a Jew, Jewish in that group.
Was it a positive experience?
I-- yes.
It was a positive experience.
Not really that much.
Not really that much, because I was a child like any other child, you know.
I went to kindergarten and then to public school.
And that was a cheder, kept me pretty busy, and just playing around with other kids.
I had no specific meaningful activities, you know.
Well, things changed dramatically, even though there was extensive anti-Semitism, considerable anti-Semitism in Austria, but it became even more-- more radical after Hitler moved in.
Well, there would be-- they had more-- there were more assaults on Jews.
There were more-- things began to happen.
Before long, in the park you could only sit on certain benches with Jew-- with Jew marked on it.
And so we're more liable to be assaulted or-- verbally and-- or physically or both.
And the other kids didn't want to play with you, the other German-- the non-Jewish kids.
I remember the class mostly [INAUDIBLE] area were Catholic.
And there was one Protestant boy in the class and he was ostracized by the Catholics.
And so he and I [INAUDIBLE] were friendly together.
But as soon as after Hitler came in and I saw him once in the streets, he wouldn't talk to me.
I said, why?
He says, because you're a Jude.
You're a Jew.
[LAUGHS]
And so I asked him why, because he refused to talk before, so he just-- he couldn't explain it.
I guess he felt that was the best thing for him to do, not to associate with Jewish children and maybe be put in that same category.
My sister being a year and a half younger, she's just a little kid.
And I guess she must have made similar observations.
We never discussed it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, when you're a kid, you take things as they are.
You don't have big psychobabble conferences, you know.
Yes.
Kristallnacht, in November 1938.
And my-- my-- where we lived, we lived right next to the haberdashery store owned by my uncle.

And next to the haberdashery store was what they called a [NON-ENGLISH], a place where they sold-- where my grandmother had a store, like a-- it was like a cafe where they sold wine, different kinds of wine.
And people just sat there on tables and chairs and they drank wine.
And so that was next to that.
And my father was-- I think my uncle may have already left for the United States, but I know he wasn't there.
But my father was helping my grandmother take the things from her store from her store into the basement, because they were looting and things like that.
And he was doing that, up and down, carrying things to the basement.
And then some police, German Austrian police came to arrest my father.
And there were a lot of Austrian people standing around.
Some of them I knew, had known us all the time, neighbors, and some I didn't.
And they took my father, the police, while he was doing this, just off the street.
And-- and-- I I ran to my father that I can I go with you?
And he said, no.
And the Germans-- the Austrians standing around thought it was very funny and they were laughing.

They found this whole scene very funny, or they got a little bit what they call in German there's a word called schadenfreude, pleasure from other people's pain, that means to be an enormous pleasure which people find when others are having-- are troubled or having pain.
And maybe for two reasons, to see other people get it.
And certainly at the other hand, it shows them that they are not getting it.
They're not getting it, so they-- but they feel good about the other guy getting it.
And they just stood around laughing and [INAUDIBLE].
They knew where he was going, you know, the adults.
And then the [INAUDIBLE] and I was trying to hold onto my father and some policeman pulled me off.
And-- and then they took him to the police station.
And from there, he went to a concentration camp in Germany There was one called-- not Dachau, another one-- one in Germany.
I can't think of the name of it.
That's where they initially took Jews to the concentration camps.
And he was there for a while.
No.
He came back.
He came out of the concentration camp a few months later.
Back to Vienna and back to the home.
From there?
From there, we still lived in the [NON-ENGLISH] Bezirk and-- which was-- which was not a Jewish neighborhood, so Jews stood out.
And there were always people making noises and-- and knocking on the windows and things like that.
And we were on the ground floor.
And also, then after a while, I think Jews had to move out of this neighborhood into Jewish-- neighborhoods they reserved for Jews.
So we moved out of that neighborhood.
Which neighborhood did you move into?
We moved into a place-- a place called [NON-ENGLISH] Bezirk.
Bezirk means district, so they had 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, no, the 20th district from the 1st district.
We got an apartment there and moved the furniture over there.
And I went to another school there.
And I think that school-- that school was also all Jewish, a public school where only Jews attended.
What year was this in?
This was in maybe latter part of '38 or '39.
Yeah.
The situation was-- with my family was my-- my grandparents moved to-- from Galicia, which was at one time a province-- a province of Austria, and then came back to Poland.
They moved from there to-- to the United States, to New York.
But my-- my mother was one year old at the time.
Her younger siblings were all born in the United States, and therefore were United States citizens.
And therefore, they had the right to move to America immediately, whereas my mother and my father, they had to take a visa and wait 'til the-- the-- the number would come up.
Parents returned from-- from America back to Vienna.
Yeah.
Then they returned-- the grandparents returned to-- from America to Vienna.
I think at that time my grandfather needed some kind of operation.
And strangely enough, the-- the-- Vienna had a higher level of medicine than in the United States at that time, back around I guess the First World War period, before the First World War period.
The-- on my mother's side, those who were American citizens, they left.
My mother had a brother and two sisters, and they and their families left for the United States.
And my grandmother left for the United States with them.
I guess she had an American citizenship.
She lived in America for a number of years.
But my mother-- but my father's side remained in Vienna.
Of course.
But you couldn't-- unless you were a citizen, you couldn't go to the United States.
They had a quota system and there were visas issued, but only a quota for so many each month.
So my parents were waiting 'til their quota number came up so they could use a visa to the United States.
But I guess there were so many applicants, and I guess a reduction in visas allowed by the United States, that there were long waiting lines and a lot of people never got out.
By the time my parents got the visa, I think around 1941, the war had already been declared, and you couldn't go through regular channels anymore.
You couldn't go like through France or through Belgium or through England.
The plan my parents had was they sent my sister and myself on what they call a kindertransport.
United Kingdom had issued visas for 10,000 Jewish children.
And so we were-- from Germany and Austria.
This was in 1939.
And the plan my parents had was that these things were really pretty rough.
Things were really very dangerous.
And they sent us on the kindertransport.
Of course, parents don't like to send small children away.
But they felt they had to to save our lives.
But the plan was hopefully that if they got their visa, they would then go to England, and on the way to America pick us up from England.
We really didn't think in those terms, just kids.
I mean, you don't think in adult terms.
Is it difficult, is it not difficult, what is the number system, you know?
They moved us into, because you-- we had to move out of-- they wanted to concentrate Jews in certain neighborhoods.
A number of months.
I don't exactly know.
A number of months, because I went to school there.
And all the kids in that school-- this was a public school, but all the kids in that school the Jewish.
And they focused the Jews in certain neighborhoods.
And I don't know if at that time they already had in mind what was happening.
But I think they were-- the beginnings were already there.
And the situations progressed with time.
I don't even know if the Nazis themselves knew what ultimate fate they had in mind for the Jews, or if they themselves had been mentally and emotionally prepared for such drastic criminal activities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nonetheless, then you had to have certain ration books for Jews and you could only shop at certain stores.
You could only sit in certain benches in parks.
And things began to sort of-- my father was sent to concentration camp.
It was more Buchenwald.
The name of the concentration camp was Buchenwald.
He was sent to Buchenwald and then a few months later he came back.
He came back.
He came back.
He looked quite-- he looked haggard and had a real hard time.
Not really.
Not-- not with the children.
Maybe with my mother and other adults, but not with two little kids at nine-- and nine and eight years old, nine and-- you know.
You know.
I guess they tried to protect-- the people tried to protect their children from the-- what was then the reality of the situation.
Well, when my father was in concentration camp naturally.
We were there with my mother.
We went to school.
We kept on going to school.
And I went and belonged to these two Jewish organizations.
And that kept me pretty busy.
And I guess the rest of the time, I was at home.
I was in school until the kindertransport, yes.
I believe I left around the 1st of September 1939.
I believe I was the last transport out of Vienna.
Anybody scheduled for the next one didn't make it, so I was very fortunate in this-- this extent.
My sister left-- left Vienna on the transport in the beginning of August.
Well, it's sort of-- it wasn't-- it was a separation.
It wasn't a separation, because we'll pick you up in a couple of months, you know.
So it's sort of like-- you follow?
It was a goodbye and then you'll see them a couple months later.
That was the-- that was the plan.
That was the hope.
And that's what they told us, so we didn't really see it as a separation.
I didn't know anybody on-- on this particular kindertransport.
I had seen-- maybe seen one or two people before.
But looking out on the train station, I saw another kid whom I'd gone to class with.
And-- and I talked to him.
I waved to him.
And subsequent-- and he also showed up.
And I don't know.
He-- so he-- I don't know what happened to him, but I saw him on the railroad station.
And he had also plans for going on-- on the transport.
But I don't know whether he-- since I was the last transport, I don't know what happened to him.
It took a long time.
It was only a matter of the railroad station going from there to Holland.
But because of the troop movements, which were-- the-- the war started on the 1st of September '39 with Poland.

And there was a considerable amount of troop movement, so what would have been a turn maybe of 10, 12 hours perhaps, or less, took a couple of days, because they were shunting our train to a side while they let the troop trains go ahead.
And then we went to Le Havre, France, a port.
And we took the ferry across to England, to Dover, England.
No.
No.
No.
N.
The troops weren't personally involved.
The-- they weren't sort of like-- I don't think they were even antagonistic to us in any way.
I-- I-- all that thing in Germany, it grew.
I don't think essentially that the German people per se were much more evil any other people.
It's just when you have an evil ideology, like Nazism, fascist Nazism or Marxist atheist communism, it's that evil ideology which leads you to evil thoughts, which in turn lead you to evil action.
And you become evil in the process.
But most of the troops there, they were sort of like an adventure, you know.
They're going to go to the front.
They're going to a war.
And you know, like young kid-- mostly young children, young, maybe 18, 19, or whatever their ages were.
And maybe even-- one of the-- I remember one of the group leaders, he was talking to them, you know.
And they were talking for-- back to him, you know, so that there was no-- we didn't see any-- I didn't feel any antagonism.
Well, maybe partially, you know.
Going on a rail-- I'm going on a train and going on a ferry, you know.
And so in a sense, partially, yes.
I wasn't scared.
There were all the other people there.
And you know, it was sort of like-- it was not like people who went to concentration camps, who were on the way to be slaughtered.
There-- that was a different atmosphere.
People knew they were on the way to be slaughtered.
The reactions of the people at the railroad stations was quite difference.
Even the people working the railroad, even the clerks in the offices of the various railroads knew they were going to slaughter.
I think this came out in the-- in the movement-- in the movie, the French movie, called where it isn't it wasn't just Mr.
Hitler and two or three of his cronies who did it, but it was entire populations who were involved in this, which of course they now deny.
It's M.
Hitler and the 20 other guys that did it, but we-- we never knew about it.

They never knew about it, even though they may have had jewelry and other things, which-- which their-- their sons and people from the out-- out in that area gave to them, to the parents and the girlfriends, which they were wearing, you know.
But They Know nothing about these things.
And they saw these Jews every day being transported, never coming back.
But they knew nothing, you know.
But they did know.
But when I left for-- for England, it was a different atmosphere and a different situation.

It was a regular train and regular, you know, carriages and regular carriages were as-- whereas the other ones transported went on cattle trucks and would be 100 pressed-- 100 pressed into 1 carriage.
We sat on regular benches in the carriage, regular carriages.
It must have been a day or two later.
Well, when I was in England, I was met by a family in London, by a family, Christian family--
Yes.
Names were Mr.
And Mrs.
Bannister.
They were country people.
They lived in a little village in West Wellow, in Hampshire, West Wellow.
And it was a very rural village.
And there was no-- and they were just people out of the goodness of their hearts wanted to take in a child.
Most of the children, a great many of them, were not taken in by Jewish families, but were taken in by non-Jewish families.

Of course, it never occurred to my parents seeing what was going on, the reactions of non-Jews to Jews, than any non-Jewish family would take in a Jewish child, so they presumed there'd be Jewish people taking care of my sister and myself.
But my sister had come about a month earlier, also to another-- to a non-Jewish family, a Christian family.
And-- and I guess they talked to these people.
Neither of these families had children of their own, and this was their chance to get a child, although-- so they talked to them.
And I guess through them, they applied for me t-- they got my name to-- to go there.
And so my sister was only a few--
About 10-- another village, another village 10 or 15 miles away.
And I used to get to see her from time to time.
Pardon?
They were both older couples.
Shortly after I got there in the village, I-- it might have even been less than a village, maybe.
What do you call that place which-- hamlet.
They may both have been hamlets rather than villages.
Mine was more of a village than the other.
Where my sister was was more of a hamlet.
And I went to school.
I got there in September.
I started school in September, the regular public school.
And I went with the other children to the public school.
And as I was going to school with-- talking to children, you learn English very rapidly.
And this was a very rural area.
We had to walk to school four miles to school.
So that was kind of--
Not-- not among those kids.
They just-- I don't even know if they really knew what a Jew was.
I don't think-- know if those kids had-- maybe there were a couple of people who had really experienced anybody being Jewish, you know, had that knowledge and experience.
Maybe one or two of the teachers had.
I don't recall.
I don't recall any-- any of that specifically.
And-- and these were good people.
And they were very good, decent to me.
OK.
